coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot-doped NiCoP and enhanced electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution ability and mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Ting
Song, Limin
Zhang, Shujuan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots/bimetal phosphide (CQDs@NiCoP) catalysts for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction were synthesized via a facile annealing method. In a 0.5 M H2SO4 system, the 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP catalyst performed well with high electrocatalytic activity, lower initial overpotential than pure NiCoP (108 vs. 182 mV) and lower Tafel slope of 80 mV/decade. The enhancement of hydrogen evolution reaction activity was probably because the addition of CQDs enhanced the catalyst conductivity and significantly accelerated charge transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction
CQDs@NiCoP
Initial overpotential
Stability
Improved mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of pure NiCoP and CQDs@NiCoP.
Characterization of the CQDs@NiCoP composite. (A, B) Low and (C, D) high magnification TEM images. (E) SAED pattern of CQDs@NiCoP.
XPS spectra of (A) and the high resolution XPS spectrum of (B) C 1s, (C) Ni 2p, (D) Co 2p and (E) P 2p for CQDs@NiCoP.

Electrochemical characterization (a) Polarization curves of black CC, pure NiCoP, CQDs@NiCoP and Pt catalyst with a scan rate of 5 mV/s in 0.5 M H2SO4. (b) The overpotential for driving a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and the current density at −0.3 V vs RHE of the catalysts with various dosage of CQDs. (c) The Tafel plots of the NiCoP, CQDs@NiCoP and Pt catalyst. (d) Nyquist plots of the pure NiCoP and 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP.

(a) Stability of the 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP catalyst with an initial polarization curve and after 3000 cycles in 0.5 M H2SO4. (b) Time dependence of catalytic current density during electrolysis for 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP at η = 216 mV.
Theoretical and detected volumes of hydrogen generated in a potentiostatic electrolysis experiment.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot-doped NiCoP and enhanced electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution ability and mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Chemical Engineering & State Key Laboratory of Hollow-Fiber Membrane Materials and Membrane Processes, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environment and Chemical Engineering & State Key Laboratory of Hollow-Fiber Membrane Materials and Membrane Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, Tianjin 300457, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots/bimetal phosphide (CQDs@NiCoP) catalysts for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction were synthesized via a facile annealing method. In a 0.5 M H2SO4 system, the 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP catalyst performed well with high electrocatalytic activity, lower initial overpotential than pure NiCoP (108 vs. 182 mV) and lower Tafel slope of 80 mV/decade. The enhancement of hydrogen evolution reaction activity was probably because the addition of CQDs enhanced the catalyst conductivity and significantly accelerated charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: Continual consumption of fossil fuels has led to gradual decline of reserves and will finally cause exhaustion, which urge humans to find out other types of affluent new energy. In the future, hydrogen is an ideal new energy that can substitute fossil fuels [1–3]. Hydrogen evolution from water electrolysis is one of the important methods to acquire hydrogen energy, but it requires a high-performance electrocatalyst for discharge reactions that provides high current under low overpotential [4]. Pt and Pt-based metals have high electrocatalytic activity, but their application is limited by high costs [5]. Thus, it is urgent to develop some durable low-overpotential, cheap and affluent catalytic materials with high hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity. Recently, many efforts have been made to develop transition metal compound (e.g. Ni, Co, Fe, Mo, V) alloys and their carbides [6,7], nitrides [8], sulfides [9] and phosphides [10–12]. In particular, transition metal phosphides with unique structures have received wide attention owing to their high catalytic activity like hydrogenases [13]. Moreover, the performance of phosphides can be further improved by metal doping [14]. This is due to the interactions between different metals can not only change the charge structure properties of each other, but also produce new synergistic effects, and may also change the coordination and overall effects of the catalyst. Thus, bimetal phosphides are usually more active than monometal phosphides [15].

As reported, hydrodesulfurization (HDS)/hydrodeoxygenation (HDO)/hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) catalysts may also be active for HER due to similar catalytic mechanisms that both the HDS and HER rely on the reversible adsorption and desorption of hydrogen on the catalyst surface [16]. Many researches found Ni2-xCoxP nanostructures have good activity for hydrodesulfurization (HDS) and hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) [17]. Thus, we may deduce that bimetal NiCoP nanostructure is also expected to be very active toward HER.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel carbon nanomaterials in sizes smaller than 10 nm, and have attracted broad attention because of strong adjustable photoluminescence ability, fast electron-transfer and large electron storage ability [18–20]. Doping with CQDs can significantly improve the catalytic performances of materials. To best of our knowledge, there is no report about the catalysts for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution of CQDs doped with bimetal phosphides. There are several ways to synthesize NiCoP. For instance, Wang et al. firstly synthesized ultrafine cobalt–nickel bimetallic phosphide powders with red phosphor [21]. Liu et al. fabricated highly active and robust Ru-doped bimetal phosphides (NiCoP@Ru) as electrocatalysts for HER in acidic and alkaline media via the one pot solid–liquid phase chemical route [22]. Zhang et al. combined HPM with CQDs by a charge-directed self-assembly strategy to fabricated CQDs/MoP for electrochemical hydrogen evolution in alkaline medium [23]. However, these synthesis methods are tedious and high cost, involving organic reagents. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple and economic synthesis route of CQDs@NiCoP for HER applications.

Based on the above discussion, here we used a new, mild and sample method to prepare CQDs@NiCoP composites and studied their HER electrochemical activity. In the approach, only a mechanical mixture of pure, solid metal salts as well as sodium hypophosphite is used to prepare the bimetal phosphides. To synthesize CQDs@NiCoP, the metal salt species were first incorporated in CQDs solution through an immersion process to synthesize metal ion-containing precursor. After a subsequent optimized phosphatizing process Afterward, the precursor and sodium hypophosphite were heated through an annealing method to form the final product. The as-synthesized 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP exhibited excellent electrocatalytic performance toward HER with exceptional overpotentials of 108 mV and 216 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, small Tafel slopes of 80 mV dec−1 in acidic solution. The improvement of HER activity might be attributed to the doped CQDs increasing charge transfer efficiency.

CQDs were synthesized by the typical electrochemical method [18]. CQDs were synthesized by the typical electrochemical method. Two graphite rods (99.99%) were inserted into ultrapure water serving as anode and cathode, and a bias of 30 V was applied between the two electrodes using a direct current power device. After a continuous stirring for 120 h, a dark yellow solution was formed in the reaction cell, and then dried. The powder formed was weighted and added to ultrapure water to form a solution of 50 mg/L.

All reagents were bought from Tianjin Chemistry Company and used without purification. CQDs@NiCoP was synthesized as follows: 0.5 g of NiCl and 0.5 g of CoCl2 were dissolved in 20 mL of various amounts of CQD solution (10, 30, 50 mg/L) under stirring for 20 min. The mixture was slowly dried by distillation in a water bath at 95 °C. The resulting precipitates were fully ground with 0.67 g of sodium hypophosphite, then put into a tube furnace, and heated at 350 °C for 2 h under a flow of H2. The products were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, washed with water and ethanol 3 times, and stored in ethanol until used.

The crystal structures were characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku D/max 2500) at λ = 1.5406, 40 kV and 40 mA. Their morphology and structures were studied on an H-7650 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Japan Hitachi) at 80 kV. The chemical states of surface elements were analyzed on a PHI5300 X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, PerkinElmer). The binding energy was calibrated by setting that of C1s as 284.8 eV.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted on a CHI650E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Inc., Shanghai) at room temperature. A tri-electrode battery (electrolyte solution = 0.5 M H2SO4) was used, in which a glass carbon electrode, a Ag/AgCl electrode and a Pt electrode were used as the working electrode, reference electrode and pair electrode, respectively. Then 4 mg of catalyst powder and the mixture containing 200 μL of ethanol, 800 μL of H2O and 30 μL of 5% Nafion were blended ultrasonically for 40 min, forming a uniform catalyst solution. Finally, catalyst and commercial 20 wt% Pt/C samples were prepared. After that, 8 mL of the catalyst solution was dripped into the 3-mm-diameter glass carbon electrode (∼loading 0.071 mg cm−2). In a H2SO4 solution, linear V-A scanning rate = 5 mV/s and cycled V-A scanning rate = 100 mV/s, accelerated aging trials were conducted after 3000 cycles of V-A scans. The linear V-A scanned curves were remeasured in comparison with the initial curve, and attenuated values were computed. The frequency range of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz, and the alternating current voltage was 5 mV. The relationship of overpotential in 0.5 M H2SO4 was computed: E(RHE) = E(Ag/AgCl) + 0.1976 V [24]. All trials were conducted at room temperature.

The XRD patterns of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP are very similar to those of pure NiCoP. Fig. 1 shows the diffraction peaks at 40.9, 44.7, 47.4, 54.9 and 75.2°, which correspond to the (1 1 1), (2 0 1), (2 1 0), (0 2 2) and (2 1 2) crystal planes, respectively, which accord with the NiCoP of pure hexagonal crystals and coincide with JCPDS (71-2336 a = 5.834 Å, b = 5.834 Å and c = 3.351 Å). The diffraction peaks are very sharp and intense, without any other impurity, indicating NiCoP was well crystallized without any impurity. Moreover, due to the little impure carbon dots, the diffraction intensity was relatively low, and no diffraction peak of carbon was observed.

Fig. 2 A and 2B shows the TEM images of CQDs@NiCoP and CQDs respectively. Clearly, the material is irregularly spherical grains in sizes of 5–50 nm, but has aggregated severely, without any regular morphology. The CQDs samples are irregularly lamellar. It may be caused by the absence of dispersion treatment after peeling off the graphite. The fine structures of these materials were further characterized by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). As showed in Fig. 2C, the crystal lattice boundaries of NiCoP show the crystal plane distance between grains is 0.22 nm, which matches with the (1 1 1) plane of hexagonal NiCoP and is consistent with the XRD patterns. Moreover, the crystal lattice distance of small grains is 0.321 nm (Fig. 2D), which is consistent with the (0 0 2) crystal plane of CQDs [25], indicating the successful doping between CQDs and NiCoP. The electron diffraction images of the zone of interest are showed in Fig. 2E. Clearly, there are multiple concentric rings, indicating the products are poly-grain materials, which are consistent with the XRD patterns.

The full-spectrum of NiCoP in Fig. 3 A implies the products consist of Ni, Co, P, C and O, in which C originates from the CQDs and O comes from the impurity introduced during the preparation. Fig. 3B-3E shows the XPS spectra of C1s, Ni 2p, Co 2p and P 2p of NiCoP, respectively. In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3B), the peak at 284.8 eV implies the sp2 hybridization of C and is ascribed to the CC bond of CQDs. The peaks at 853.1 and 856.4 eV (Fig. 3C) correspond to the Ni 2p3/2 of Ni-P and oxidized Ni species, while the one at 870.9 eV is ascribed to Ni 2p1/2. The peaks at 778.2 and 781.2 eV (Fig. 3D) correspond to the Co 2p3/2 of Co-Ni and oxidized Co species, while the one at 794.2 eV is ascribed to Co 2p1/2. The peaks at 133.3 and 129.8 eV (Fig. 3E) are attributed to the P 2p1/2 and P 2p3/2 of NiCoP, respectively. These results are consistent with some reports that the P 2p of transition metal phosphides on the high-resolution XPS spectra usually splits into two peaks at P 2p1/2 and P 2p3/2 [17,26–27]. The P with binding energy of 129.8 eV is mainly ascribed to the P bound from Ni and Co, and the peak with large binding energy of 133.3 eV may result from the oxidized P on the surface of NiCoP after contact with air [28]. The Ni 2p3/2 has higher binding energy than Ni of NiO (853.1 vs. 852.3 eV), Co 2p3/2 has higher binding energy than CoO (777.9 eV) [29], P 2p has lower binding energy than P (129.8 vs. 130.2 eV) [11], so Ni and Co both carry a part of positive charge (δ+) and P carries partial negative charge (δ-), which indicates the transfer of electron density from Ni and Co to P. The oxygen signal was likely related to some oxides or simple absorbed water on the surface of the CQDs@NiCoP nanoparticles, as a thin layer of oxides are commonly observed on the surface of many nonoxide materials [10].

The activity of pure NiCoP and CQDs@NiCoP were studied in a typical three-electrode setup with 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte. Then the blank glass carbon electrode and commercial 20 %wt Pt/C were compared. All polarization curves were calibrated by iR. As showed on the polarization curves in Fig. 4 a, the Pt/C catalyst shows the HER activity as expected and is close to zero overpotential, while the performance of the bare glass carbon electrode almost can be ignored. In addition, wealso prepared another two samples with different dosage of CQDs and investigated the HER activities by the overpotential for driving a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and the current density at −0.3 V vs RHE in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. In Fig. 4a, the 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP catalyst performed the smallest overpotential of 108 mV to drive a current density of 10 mA cm−2, about 74 mV more positive than that of pure NiCoP (182 mV). The current density at −0.3 V vs RHE for these samples increased in the sequence of NiCoP < 10 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP < 50 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP < 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP (as CQDs@NiCoP). From the results above, we could suggest that the optimal dosage of CQDs was 30 mg/L. Furthermore, compared with pure NiCoP, CQDs@NiCoP catalyst exhibits higher HER ability with overpotential of 216 mV and 297 at 10 mA cm−2 and 100 mA cm−2. However, pure NiCoP requires 316 mV to reach a current density of 10 mA cm−2.

The Tafel curves of phosphide and platinum catalysts are showed in Fig. 4c, which accord with the Tafel equation η = blog(j) + a, where j is current density, b is Tafel slope, and a is the intercept relative to exchanged current density. The Tafel slope is the inherent property of an electrocatalyst determined from a speed-controlling step of HER. Pure NiCoP shows a Tafel slope of 122 mV/dec, but that of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP decreases to 80 mV/dec. These results further confirm 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP has higher HER catalytic activity than pure NiCoP, indicating the modified electrode 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP has excellent reaction dynamics.
The process of HER can be uncovered from the perspective of Tafel slope, and generally is divided into two steps:

A discharge step (Volmer reaction: H3O+ + e− → Hads + H2O), and a desorption step (Heyrovsky reaction: Hads + H3O+ + e− → H2 + H2O) or recombination step (Tafel reaction: Hads + Hads → H2), where Hads indicates the hydrogen atom is at an active position of the catalyst. The speed-controlling step of HER was identified by analyzing the Tafel slope. It was found the speed-controlling steps at Tafel slope of 116, 38 or 29 mV/Dec were Volmer, Heyrovsky and Tafel, respectively. The Tafel slope of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP is 80 mV/Dec following the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism [30], and the discharge speed and desorption speed during HER are nearly equal [31].

To study the electrode dynamics under catalytic conditions of HER, we analyzed the electrochemical impedance spectra of pure NiCoP and 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP. The Nyquist curves in Fig. 4d show 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP had much low charge transfer resistance (Rct) than pure NiCoP. The small Rct of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP suggests excellent electron-transfer ability and indicates this composite is surely an efficient HER electrocatalyst.

Another key factor affecting catalytic performance is durability. The durability of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP was analyzed by continuous recycled V-A scans in acid media. After 3000 cycles of CV measurement, compared with the initial CV of CQD in NiCoP, overpotential only increased slightly (Fig. 5 a), indicating the excellent electrochemical durability in 0.5 M H2SO4. To further study the stability of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP in electrocatalytic HER, we measured the current-time curve (without iR calibration) at the overpotential of 216 mV vs. RHE. As showed in Fig. 5b, the initial current density of the catalyst after 10 h only decreased by 25%. The attenuation of current density may be due to the slow mass transfer of the electrolyte or the gas desorption on electrode surface in HER [10]. These results confirm the high stability of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP in long-term electrochemical process.

Fig. 6 compares the theoretical and detected volumes of hydrogen generated during potentiostatic electrolysis. The theoretical volumes were computed by assuming that all electrons passing through the circuit participate in the reduction reaction of H+ (2H+ + 2e− → H2), and the experimental volume was detected by the water displacement method. The experimental and theoretical volumes are in good accordance, indicating ≈100% Faradic efficiency of the catalytic reaction.

The excellent catalytic activity of 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP can be explained from two aspects. (1) Computation via the density functional theory shows Co doping can change the electron structure of Ni2P and further optimize the capture and desorption of atomic hydrogen [29]. (2) The small current density of the GCE only loaded with NiCoP may be attributed to the low conductivity between NiCoP nanoparticles that slows down electron transfer, while the addition of CQDs largely accelerates the electron transfer between the electrode and its surface compound catalyst, which significantly enhances the HER performance.

In this work, CQDs@NiCoP electrocatalysts for HER were synthesized via an easy low-temperature roasting method. 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP showed higher electrocatalytic performance in HER than pure NiCoP (182 mV), with initial overpotential 108 mV, small Tafel slope of 80 mV/decade, and was stable in acid media. The high charge transfer efficiency contributed to improving the activity of HER. 30 mg/L CQDs@NiCoP had much lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) than pure NiCoP. This study offers a potential doping method, and acquires high-performance and low-cost electrocatalysts via the introduction of new metal element or other heteroatom.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042).
